coredata.dc:title: A highly efficient, rapid, room temperature synthesis method for coal-based water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots and its use in Fe3+ ion detection

coredata.dc:description: We report a method for the of coal-based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) at room temperature using a mixture of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and formic acid (HCOOH) as the oxidant instead of concentrated HNO3 or H2SO4. The CDs have an excitation dependent behavior with a high quantum yield (QY) of approximately 7.2%. The CDs are water soluble and have excellent photo-stability, good resistance to salt solutions, and are insensitive to pH in a range of 2.0-12.0. The CDs were used as a very sensitive probe for the turn-off sensing of Fe3+ ion with a detection limit as low as 600 nmol/L and a detection range from 2 to 100 μmol/L. This work provides a way for the high value-added utilization of coal.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: NEW CARBON MATERIALS Volume 38, Issue 6, Dec. 2023 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: New Carbon Materials, 2023,38(6):1104-1115. Received date: 07 Jul. 2020; Revised date: 20 Agu. 2020 *Corresponding author E-mail: jxguo1012@163.com; CopyrightÂ©2023, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-5805(23)60706-1 Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version. RESEARCH PAPER A highly efficient, rapid, room temperature synthesis method for coal-based water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots and its use in Fe3+ ion detection Zhong-fu Cheng1, Xue-yan Wu2, Lei Liu1, Long He1, Zu-guo Yang1, Chang Cao1, Yan Lu2, Ji-xi Guo2,* 1Sinopec Northwest Oilfield Branch Company, Urumqi 830011, China 2Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region; Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education; College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, China Abstract: We report a method for the of coal-based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) at room temperature using a mixture of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and formic acid (HCOOH) as the oxidant instead of concentrated HNO3 or H2SO4. The CDs have an excitation dependent behavior with a high quantum yield (QY) of approximately 7.2%. The CDs are water soluble and have excellent photo-stability, good resistance to salt solutions, and are insensitive to pH in a range of 2.0-12.0. The CDs were used as a very sensitive probe for the turn-off sensing of Fe3+ ion with a detection limit as low as 600 nmol/L and a detection range from 2 to 100 Î¼mol/L. This work provides a way for the high value-added utilization of coal. Key Words: Coal; Carbon dots; Synthesis; Photoluminescent; Fe3+ ion detection 1 Introduction Carbon nanomaterials are a class of low-dimensional materials that have aroused a great deal of interest in the past 30 years since the discoveries of fullerene[1], carbon nanotubes (CNTs)[2] and grapheme[3]. Carbon dots (CDs), also known as the carbon nanodots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or graphene quantum dots (GQDs), represent another kind of carbon nanomaterials besides fullerene, CNTs and graphene. Compared with fullerene, CNTs and graphene, the CDs have just started receiving rising interest in the past decade, which can be accredited to a serendipitous finding of fluorescent carbonaceous nanoparticles during a standard preparative electrophoresis experiment for purifying arc-discharged SWCNTs in 2004[4]. In 2006, Sun et al. obtained fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by chemical synthesis via laser ablation of a carbon target and surface passivation, which were later referred to as CDs[5]. From then on, researches on CDs have become a hot topic in chemistry and many related fields owing to the chemical inertness, lack of optical blinking, resistance to photo-bleaching, stable fluorescence emission, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, hydrophilic nature and ease of functionalization. Based on the above excellent properties, CDs are versatile and can be used in a wide range of technologies, such as bio-imaging and metal ion sensing, photocatalysis, photovoltaic devices and energy conversion/storage devices[6-11]. CDs have been synthesized or fabricated from various carbon-based materials including fullerene[12], graphite or graphene oxides[13-14], CNTs[15], carbon fibers[16], petroleum coke[17], amino acids[18], urine[19], carbon black[20], chitosan[21], orange and apple juice[22], pomelo peel[23], watermelon peel[24] and vegetables[25] by several existing techniques such as laser ablation[5], pyrolysis[26], wet oxidation[27-28], ultrasound- and microwave-assisted synthesis[29] and hydrothermal synthesis[30- 31]. In practice, the chemical oxidation, hydrothermal or solvothermal treatment are under harsh conditions, such as use of concentrated HNO3 or H2SO4 at high temperatures for a long time. Although the above mentioned routes provide approaches to the synthesis of CDs, the precursors are of high prices, such as fullerene, graphite, CNTs and carbon fibers. From the previously reported precursors, it can be concluded that carbon materials with graphite or graphene structures are the possible precursors of CDs. Coal is indeed a cheap and abundant fossil energy resource being consumed maily to obtain energy in the past hundred years. Because the angstrom or nanometer-sized crystalline carbon domains are too small to access, its further use to fabricate new carbon materials is limited[32-35]. Hence, it seems that the coal would be a perfect precursor for the synthesis of CDs, and it would be of great significance if CDs could be finally obtained from coal through some simple oxidation treatments[35-39]. But efficient one-step strategy to fabricate CDs on a large scale is still challenging in this field. Herein, a facile and high yield strategy for preparating CDs at room temperature is proposed by using coal as a Zhong-fu Cheng et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(6): 1104-1115 precursor and the mixture of HCOOH and H2O2 as oxidants. This strategy is suitable for large-scale production without external heating or any additional energy input. The mixture of HCOOH and H2O2 as oxidants is used to synthesize the coal- based CDs with the safe nature of its degradation products, mostly carbon dioxide, oxygen and water without any toxic gases such as NO2 and N2O4 generated during the process. In addition, the oxidizing reaction is performed under room temperature. Thus, the efficient synthesis process of coal-based CDs not only avoids using concentrated acid (HNO3 or H2SO4) and introducing metal impurities, but also does not need any other post-processing steps except centrifugation. Similar to this method, the small crystalline carbons in coal could be exfoliated with a mixture of HCOOH and H2O2 and then the nanoparticles undergo self-assembly[40]. The structures of CDs were investigated by a series of characterization methods. Moreover, the as-prepared CDs were used to detect Fe3+. and the lowest detection limit (LOD) was 600 nmol/L, which is superior to many other research studies. This fast and efficient method for the synthesis of coal-based CDs will not only provide high value-added utilization of coal, but also have potential application value in cell imaging and safety detection. 2 Experimental 2.1 Chemicals and materials Coal was obtained from Heishan, Xinjiang, China. 30% H2O2 and HCOOH were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (SCRC) Shanghai, China and Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory, (Tianjin, China), respectively. Other regents of analytical grade were obtained and used as received without any further purification. Doubly distilled water (18 Mï�—) was used in all of the experiments. 2.2 Synthesis of CDs The coal sample without pretreatment was crushed and sieved with a 200 mesh sieve. 200 mg coal was dispersed in 25 mL HCOOH and then mixed with 5 mL of 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution. The obtained mixture was stirred at 600 r/min with a magnetic stirring apparatus for 12 h in the ambient condition without any external heating. After unreacted powder was removed by centrifugation at 8 000 r/min for 10 min, the suspension containing CDs was obtained. The excess reaction solution was removed in vacuum by a rotary evaporator and the excess HCOOH was reused. The control experiments were also conducted. 2.3 Fe3+ detection The Fe3+ selective detection and sensitivity experiments were performed in aqueous solutions. The CDs aqueous solution (0.2 mg/mL) was served as the blank solution. Different amounts of aqueous metal ions solution (1 mmol/L) (Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Hg2+ and Cr3+) were added to the CDs blank solution to evaluate the influence of metal ions on the photoluminescence of CDs and assess the selectivity for Fe3+. Then the sensitivity towards Fe3+ was also investigated under the different Fe3+ concentrations in the range (0 to 200 Î¼mol/L) at pH=6.8. The photoluminescence spectra of the smooth and well blended mixture solutions were collected at excitation wavelength of 320 nm. All photoluminescence measurements were carried out at room temperature. 2.4 Characterization of the CDs The morphology of the samples was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). High resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images of the products were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2010F electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The infrared spectroscopy was carried out using a Bruker VERTEX- 70 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer in a wavenumber range from 400 to 4 000 cmâˆ’1. Raman spectroscopy was performed on a Bruker SENTERRA confocal microscope Raman spectrometer using a 532 nm laser. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted on a Bruker D8 Advance Diffraction diffractometer in the range from 10Â° to 80Â°, with Cu KÎ± radiation (Î¸ = 0.154 05 nm) at 40 kV and 40 mA. NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker ADVANCE III 400 MHz spectrometer (13C NMR: 100 MHz) in D2O. UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a HITACHI U-3900H and the Fluorescence spectra were performed on a HITACHI F-4500 Fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out by using a FluoroLog-3 spectrophotometer (Horibaâ€“Jobinâ€“Yvon, Edison, NJ, USA). Quantum yield (QY) was measured according to established procedure by employing quinine sulfate in 0.10 mol/L H2SO4 solution (literature quantum yield 54% at 350 nm) as the standard. The QY of CDs in water was calculated according to: ð�›·f i = ð��¹ið�‘“sð�‘›i 2 ð��¹sð�‘“ið�‘›s 2ð�›·f s (1) Where ð�›·f i and ð�›·f s are the photoluminescence QY of the sample and the standard, respectively. The subscript f is used because in most cases one is dealing with fluorescence. ð��¹ð�‘– and ð��¹ð�‘ are the integrated intensities (areas) of sample and standard spectra, respectively. ð�‘“ð�‘¥ is the absorption factor, the fraction of the light impinging on the sample that is absorbed (ð�‘“ð�‘¥= 1â€“10â€“Ax, where A = absorbance). The absorption factor must be accurately determined for solutions with a low absorbance, typically A < 0.05, in order to avoid internal filter effects and errors arising from uneven distribution of the excited species in the detected volume. The refractive indices of the sample and reference solution are ni and ns, respectively. 3 Results and discussion The synthesis schematic of coal-based CDs is shown in Fig. 1. These CDs show uniform size, good dispersion and excellent water solubility. HCOOH, a simplest carboxylic acid, is an important intermediate in chemical synthesis. H2O2, an environmentally friendly oxidant, was selected as the oxidant in our strategy due to its obvious advantages. First of all, the peroxyformic acid (HCOOOH), which was formed from the Zhong-fu Cheng et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(6): 1104-1115 mixture of HCOOH and H2O2 at room temperature, has high reactivity and strong oxidizing power to oxidize and exfoliate the small crystalline carbons in coal effectively. Thus, it avoids employing concentrated HNO3 or H2SO4 to get strong oxidation. The next, H2O2 is a green oxidant, which will not produce the toxic gases to cause environmental risks. Once more, the elemental compositions of the mixture of HCOOH and H2O2 are only C, H and O, which avoids introducing metal ion impurities. Last but not least, our strategy does not need external heating or any additional energy input. The contrast experiments (Fig. S1 and Table S1) indicated that the fluorescent CDs canâ€™t be observed in pure HCOOH and H2O2 environments. The yield of CDs relies on the blending ratio of HCOOH to H2O2 and reaction time. The changes in the blending ratios of HCOOH to H2O2 and reaction time does not affect the fluorescent peak wavelength and the excitation- dependent fluorescence behaviors (Fig. S2 and S3). What is more, the excess HCOOH can be recycled. Compared with previous reported strategies (Table 1), it is the most green and mild synthesis method for CD synthesis from coal to date. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to investigate the morphology of the as-made CDs. The TEM image shows that the sizes of nanoparticles in suspension are mainly from 1.45 to 3.25 nm and the average value is 2.2 nm (Fig. 2a and 2a insert). The most particles are observed in high- resolution TEM (HRTEM) image to be crystalline carbon particles possessing well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of ca. 0.21 nm, which is close to the (100) diffraction facets of graphite carbon (Fig. 2b), the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) image shows the high crystallinity of the CDs. (Fig. 2b insert)[14, 41-42]. With such a high carbon- lattice-structure content, however, no obvious G band at 1598 cmâˆ’1 and D band at 1 350 cmâˆ’1 are detected in the Raman spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S4), which may be attributed to the high fluorescence that disturbs the Raman characterization. XRD was performed to investigate the structure of CDs, Fig. S5 shows that the raw coal has a wide (002) and (100) peaks of graphitic carbon at around 24Â° and 42Â°, respectively while CDs only exhibit a broader (002) diffraction peak at 25.7Â° in the whole range, which is attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms[6]. Compared with the raw coal the XRD peak for the as-synthesized CDs is shifted to a higher degree, indicating that the CDs have a smaller interlayer spacing than raw coal. The smaller interlayer spacing in CDs can be ascribed to the formation of hydrogen bonds among the oxygen-containing groups at the edges of graphene layers in CDs[43-44]. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis reveals the functional groups present on the CD surface. The spectra show the presence of C-C/C=C, C-O/C=O, C-H and O-H vibration modes for the raw coal (Fig. 3)[35]. The CDs exhibit peaks at around 3 420 cmâ€“1, which corresponds to O-H vibrations. The peaks at 1 380 and 1 141 cmâ€“1 are assigned to C-N stretching and N-H wagging, respectively. Furthermore, the peaks at 1 633 and 1 717 cmâ€“1 are observed, which indicates the presence of C=C and C=O bending vibration, respectively[45-46]. The surface groups were investigated by XPS analysis (Fig. 4), the XPS survey spectrum confirms the presence of C, O and N with atomic concentrations of 56.42%, 40.26% and 3.32% (Table S2), respectively. The high-resolution XPS-peak deconvolution analysis gives 4 peaks for C1s that are attributed to 4 chemical states for carbon in CDs (Fig. 4b and Table S3). A peak at 284.78 eV represents the C-C, 286.98 eV belongs to C-N bonding, 285.98 eV corresponds to C-O and 288.88 eV is due to C=O. The peak located at 399.98 eV can be ascribed to the tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms in the form of pyrrole- like N[47-48], and the peak centered at 401.78 eV due to the surface â€“NH2 groups (Fig. 4c)[21, 49]. The O1s spectrum of CDs can be divided into 2 peaks at 531.78 and 533.18 eV, indicating the existence of C-O and C=O (Fig. 4d), respectively[50]. All of the results presented above including TEM, Raman, XRD, FTIR and XPS results confirm that CDs have been prepared by a simple and efficient synthetic process. The optical properties of CDs are discussed below. Centrifugalization 10% [38] Coal DMF, 180 Â°C, 12 h. Centrifugalization 26% [39] Coal HCOOH /30% H2O2 (5/1, v/v)12 h, r.t. Centrifugalization 54% This work Zhong-fu Cheng et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(5): 1104-1115 Fig. 2 (a) TEM image of CDs showing a regular size and size distribution. (b) HRTEM image of a representative CDs particle Fig. 3 FTIR spectra of raw coal and as-made CDs For the application of CDs in biosensors and bio-imaging the biggest challenges are the optical and water-soluble properties. Thus, the optical and hydrophilic properties were further investigated. Here we selected 4 types of organic solvents with different hydrophobic properties, CH2Cl2, ethyl acetate (EA), CHCl3 and n-hexane, to study the hydrophilic properties of the as-synthesized CDs. As shown in Fig. S6, after the as-synthesized CD aqueous solution was added into the CH2Cl2, ethyl acetate (EA), CHCl3 and n-hexane, the CDs were only dispersed in water, forming a transparent, uniform and light yellow solution at day light. The aqueous layer shows a bright blue fluorescence under irradiation of a hand-held ultraviolet lamp with 365 nm UV light. The above results indicate that the excellent hydrophilic properties of CDs. Fig. 5a depicts the typical UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CDs with no obvious absorption peaks, which is likely due to the distribution of different electron transitions[51-52]. The absorbance of the CDs decreases with decreasing the CD concentration (Fig. S7). When excited at 320 nm, the CDs show a fluorescence emission peak at 445 nm with a high QY of approximately 7.2% (Table S4). A bright blue emission color is observed under a hand-held UV lamp (Fig. 5a insert). The maximum excitation band is mirror symmetric with the maximum emission band. Fig. 5b presents the fluorescence Fig. 4 (a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s of CDs Zhong-fu Cheng et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(5): 1104-1115 Fig. 5 (a) UV-Vis and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CDs, the insets in (a) are photographs of the CDs aqueous solution taken under day light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (b) The corresponding fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 520 nm, (CCDs = 0.05 mg/mL) emission spectra of CDs, which were measured under different excitation wavelengths. The excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of CDs is observed obviously, agreeing well with the CDs in previous reported literatures[53-55]. The excitation- dependent emission may be linked with the aromatic Cï¼�C bonds and surface defects caused by the C-OH and Cï¼�O groups that affect the band gap of CDs. In addition, the surface state is akin to a molecular state whereas the size effect is a result of quantum dimensions, both of which lead to the complexity of the excited states of CDs[10, 38, 56-57]. The fluorescence of as-prepared CDs was measured under various concentrations. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity and wavelength of CDs are dependent on the CDs concentration. The fluorescence is quenched by increasing the concentration and the emission peak is red shifted, which can be attributed to the aggregation of CDs particles, leading to a decrease of the band gaps of CDs in the aqueous solutions[37, 58]. The self-quenching phenomena take place in high concentrations due to the aggregation quenching effect[9, 36]. Also, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was studied under various ionic strengths of NaCl at long-time irradiation with UV light and different pH values. Fig. S9 exhibits that the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains unchanged with the NaCl molar concentration increased from 0 to 2.0 mol/L. The result indicates that the as-synthesized CDs have good salinity tolerance. We also observed that the fluorescence intensity does not change even after continuous excitation with a 12 W UV light lamp for 60 min (Fig. S10), confirming that the as-made CDs possess the robust photo-stability. Furthermore, the CDs still show strong storage and transportation stability even after stored in the aqueous solution at room temperature for 35 days (Fig. S11) and repeatedly dispersed in water for 10 times (Fig. S12). The changes of pH values from 2.0 to 12.0 have little influence on the fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. S13), which is favorable for applications in vivo environments. It is ascribed to the structure of CDs lack of free zigzag sites, which can be protonated under acidic conditions and restored under alkaline conditions[59]. All above results show that CDs have a great stability. As we all know, the surface of CDs has oxygen-containing functional groups, which not only can facilitate their water solubility, but also provide a possibility as a probe for metal ion detection because of the coordination effect between the oxygen functional groups and metal ions[60-62]. The potential utility of CDs in fluorescence-based metal ion sensing was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6a, the photoluminescence intensity of CDs is obviously quenched by Fe3+ when excited with 320 nm light, whilst there is no quenching of CD emission when metal ions Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Hg2+ and Cr3+ are added at the concentration of 500 ï�­mol/L. Only Cu2+ and Pb2+ show relatively weak fluorescence quenching, which can be attributed to the nonspecific interactions between the carboxylic groups and the metal ions[39, 63]. The selective and sensitive response of CDs to Fe3+ is due to the presence of a large number of oxygen containing functional groups particularly carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in CDs. The result indicates that CDs can be developed as an ideal probe for detecting Fe3+. In another control experiment, 150 ï�­mol/L of Fe3+ alone (red bars, Fig. 6b) and the mixtures of 150 ï�­mol/L of Fe3+ and 150 ï�­mol/L of the above mentioned metal ions (blue bars, Fig. 6b) were added into the CDs aqueous solution to examine the quenching effects. The interference tests show that the coexistence of other ions only has a small or negligible effect on the fluorescence quenching. To evaluate in detail the potential of CDs for detecting Fe3+ in aqueous solution, different amounts of Fe3+ were added. With the addition of Fe3+, the luminescence intensity of CDs at 450 nm gradually decreases (Fig. 6c). The F0/F is dependent on the concentrations of Fe3+ (Fig. S14), and F0/F ratios have a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.996 4) with the Fe3+ concentration ranging from 2 to 100 ï�­mol/L (Fig. 6d). The limit of detection Zhong-fu Cheng et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(5): 1104-1115 Fig. 6 (a) Selectivity of the as-made CDs as a probe for metal ions in water, at the metal ions concentration of 500 Î¼mol/L. (b) Interference experiments of the aqueous CD solution towards Fe3+ (red bars) and other metal ions (blue bars). (c) Photoluminescence spectra of the CDs at various concentrations of Fe3+ from 0 to 200 Î¼mol/L from top to bottom. (d) The emission intensity ratio F0/F depends on the concentration of the added Fe3+, which is derived from the results of (c). Here, F and F0 are the luminescence intensities of CDs at 450 nm in the presence and absence of Fe3+ ion, respectively. The concentration of the CDs is 0.2 mg/mL, (Î»ex = 320 nm, Î»em = 450 nm) (LOD) is estimated to be 600 nmol/L according to the equation: LOD = 3.0 (SD/S). Here, S and SD are the signal-to-noise ratio and standard deviation (SD) of the response, respectively. The detection sensitivity of Fe3+ is superior to some previously reported literatures (Table S5). Therefore, the above results significantly indicate that the synthesized fluorescent CDs have potential application in Fe3+ detection in practice. The fluorescent tuning on and off can be achieved by many kinds of mechanisms, such as photo-induced electron transfer (PET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), electronic energy transfer (EET) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). However, the above mechanisms only work when they have a particular distance, geometric structure or electronic property. The fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs, inner filter effect (IFE)[64], aggregation caused quenching (ACQ)[39], or electron transfer[65-66], are determined by its special structure. In order to obtain the possible mechanism of Fe3+ detection, the UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy, and fluorescence decay of CDs were performed. As shown in Fig. 7a, the absorption spectra of CDs show no obvious change before and after Fe3+ is added, which indicates that no coordination or coordination complexes is present between them[61, 67-68]. As demonstrated in Fig. 7b, the absorption spectra show that Fe3+ exhibits a broad absorption ranging from 200 to 550 nm, which not only will block some or all of the excitation light through (320 nm) but also absorbs the emission light (450 nm) emitted from CDs. Moreover, Fig. 7c depicts that there is no obvious variation in the fluorescence decay curves of CDs with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, and the lifetimes are 7.79, 7.40 and 7.33 ns of Fe3+ at concentrations of 0, 100 and 200 ï�­mol/L (Table S6), respectively. The nearly invariable lifetime indicates that no ultrafast CDs/Fe3+ ion electron-transfer process and no available dynamic quenching process occur [39, 65, 69-70]. All Zhong-fu Cheng et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(5): 1104-1115 Fig. 7 (a) UV-Vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ and CDs in absence and presence of Fe3+. (b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs and UV-Vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs in absence and presence of Fe3+ measured with excitation and emission wavelengths of 340 and 450 nm, respectively. The concentration of the CDs is 0.2 mg/mL (Î»ex = 320 nm, Î»em = 450 nm) above results manifest that the fluorescence quench is mainly attributed to the IFE of Fe3+ on the fluorescence emission of CDs[64, 71]. Therefore, the strong absorbance of Fe3+ could be easily associated with fluorescence quenching of CDs, which guarantee the IFE occurrence can be done in a productive way. 4 Conclusions Water-soluble fluorescent CDs derived from coal have been successfully synthesized at room temperature through a green, simple and one-pot strategy using the mixture of H2O2 and HCOOH as oxidants and the production yield is over 54%. The as-made CDs have a uniform size, good dispersion and excellent water solubility, and a high QY of 7.2%. Moreover, the synthesized CDs exhibit robust photo-stability, good resistance to salt solution, insensitive to pH in a wide range of 2.0-12.0, high specificity and selectivity for Fe3+ with a detection limit as low as 600 nmol/L. Therefore, this work could pave a novel way for producing high quality CDs from coal and endowing them with many new potential applications. Last, the works of the general validity of the synthesis method and the modified PL performance of CDs made from coal by co-doping heteroatom, such as B/N/S, are in progress now, and the results will soon be reported. head.ce:title: A highly efficient, rapid, room temperature synthesis method for coal-based water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots and its use in Fe3+ ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sinopec Northwest Oilfield Branch Company, Urumqi 830011, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sinopec Northwest Oilfield Branch Company, Urumqi 830011, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region; Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education; College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials
Autonomous Region; Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry
Ministry of Education; College of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region; Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education; College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a method for the of coal-based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) at room temperature using a mixture of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and formic acid (HCOOH) as the oxidant instead of concentrated HNO3 or H2SO4. The CDs have an excitation dependent behavior with a high quantum yield (QY) of approximately 7.2%. The CDs are water soluble and have excellent photo-stability, good resistance to salt solutions, and are insensitive to pH in a range of 2.0-12.0. The CDs were used as a very sensitive probe for the turn-off sensing of Fe3+ ion with a detection limit as low as 600 nmol/L and a detection range from 2 to 100 μmol/L. This work provides a way for the high value-added utilization of coal.
